Alternatively, once 417 lands, \(\mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}\, X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\, X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric) \(\mathrm{MonoidalCategory}\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\), and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton, \(\operatorname{Pic}(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}\, X))\), exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\, R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\, R))\) and transports \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on all invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction already supplies.

LaTeX
Lean
  • AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup
  • AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.picCommGroup
Lemma 422

[Closure of \(\otimes \) on the locally-trivial carrier \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)] Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The carrier is the locally-trivial subtype

\[ \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\, \pi _C\, \pi _T \; \; =\; \; \bigl\{ \, M \in \mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times _S T) \, \bigm |\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\, M \, \bigr\} \]

introduced in 18. The bifunctor \(\otimes _{C \times _S T}\) of 404 restricts to this subtype: if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\, M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\, M'\) then \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes _{C \times _S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes \) closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\, \pi _C\, \pi _T\), supplying the binary operation the relative group law multiplies with.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 411

[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial] Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let \(L, L' \in \mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}\, X\) be locally-trivial modules of rank one (i.e. line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes _X L'\) of 403 is again locally trivial of rank one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes _X\) restricts to a bifunctor on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\, X \subset \mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}\, X\) of locally-trivial rank-one modules.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 407

[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion] Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}\, X\) be arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison morphism

\[ (M \otimes _X N)\big|_f \; \xrightarrow {\; \; }\; M\big|_f \; \otimes _U\; N\big|_f \]

is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The reason is structural to open immersions — restriction along \(f\) is base change along the structure-sheaf isomorphism induced by \(f\), not along a general ring map — and base change along a ring isomorphism is trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 419Left and right unitors for \(\otimes _X\)

Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}\, X\) an arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms

\[ \mathcal{O}_X \otimes _X M \; \xrightarrow {\ \sim \ }\; M, \qquad M \otimes _X \mathcal{O}_X \; \xrightarrow {\ \sim \ }\; M . \]
LaTeX
Lean
  • AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor
  • AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor
Lemma 512Naturality of the terminal ring map

The restriction map \(R(g) : R(X) \to R(Y)\) along \(g : X \to Y\) carries \(\mathtt{termRingMap}_X\, f\) to \(\mathtt{termRingMap}_Y\, f\), so the images of a global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) are compatible with restriction.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 513The terminal ring map at the terminal object is the identity

At the terminal object itself the induced ring map \(R(T) \to R(T)\) is the identity, since the unique \(T \to T\) is \(\mathrm{id}_T\).

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 618

[Quasi-coherent module on an affine open is tilde of its sections (Stacks 01I8)] Source: Stacks 01I8 (a quasi-coherent module on an affine scheme is \(\widetilde M\) for some module). Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal N\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-module, and \(V \subset X\) an affine open. Then the pullback of \(\mathcal N\) along hV.fromSpec is canonically isomorphic to tilde of its sections:

\[ (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\, \mathtt{hV.fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\, \mathcal N \; \cong \; \widetilde{\, \Gamma (\mathcal N, V)\, } \quad \text{(on $\mathrm{Spec}\, \Gamma (X, V)$).} \]

Mathlib b80f227 ships the analog for free quasi-coherent modules (isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation partial), but the general statement “\([N.IsQuasicoherent] \Rightarrow N|_V \cong \widetilde{\Gamma (N,V)}\) on an affine open \(V\)” (Stacks 01I8 lemma-affine-qc-module-iff) is unowned at the Scheme.Modules layer.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 130

[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization] Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf restriction map

\[ \Gamma (\widetilde M,\, \top ) \longrightarrow \Gamma (\widetilde M,\, D(b)), \]

read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma (\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma (\widetilde M, \top )\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement (3) of the proof of Lemma 134.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 544Sectionwise left-whiskering is a tensor map

The underlying additive map of \((F \triangleleft g)_X\) is \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\, (F(X))\, g_X\).

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 543Sectionwise left-whiskering on a simple tensor

The underlying additive map of \((F \triangleleft g)_X\) sends \(a \otimes b\) to \(a \otimes g_X(b)\).

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 754Cotangent image of \(x \in \mathfrak m \setminus \mathfrak m^2\)

For a local ring \((R, \mathfrak m)\) and \(x \in \mathfrak m\) with \(x \notin \mathfrak m^2\), the image of \(x\) in the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) is nonzero. This is the positivity input for the cotangent dimension-drop.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 463

[Composition coherence of the pushforward unit comparison] Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes. Write \(\mathrm{upu}\) for the pushforward (right-adjoint) unit comparison \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\), the canonical map \(\mathcal{O} \to f_*(\mathcal{O})\) comparing the structure sheaf of the target with the pushforward of the structure sheaf of the source. Then \(\mathrm{upu}\) satisfies the composition coherence

\[ \mathrm{upu}(h \mathbin {;} f) \; =\; \mathrm{upu}\, f \; \mathbin {;}\; (\mathtt{pushforward}\, f).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{upu}\, h\bigr) \; \mathbin {;}\; (\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\, h\, f).\mathrm{hom}, \]

where \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\, h\, f : (h \mathbin {;} f)_* \cong f_* \circ h_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality isomorphism of the pushforward. Sectionwise both sides are nothing but the functoriality of the structure-sheaf ring maps, so the identity holds on the nose after the comparison of sections. This is the right-adjoint half of the unit composition coherence, and it is the input consumed by the mate transport that produces the pullback-side coherence 464.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 427

[d.1-bridge: locally bijective implies stalkwise iso] Let \(X\) be a topological space and \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on the small site \(\mathtt{Opens}\, X\), equipped with its canonical Grothendieck topology \(J\) satisfying \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\, \mathtt{Ab}\). Let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued presheaves lies in \(J.W\). Then for every point \(x \in X\) the induced \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map

\[ (\mathtt{stalkFunctor}\, \mathtt{Ab}\, x).\mathtt{map}\, \bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\, g\bigr) \; \colon \; M_x \; \longrightarrow \; N_x \]

is an isomorphism. Conversely, if the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism at every point, then \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\, g \in J.W\).

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 547Flat right-whiskering preserves the localizer

For sectionwise-flat \(F\) and \(g \in J.W\), the right-whiskered \(g \triangleright F\) again lies in \(J.W\).

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 548Flatness-free right-whiskering preserves the localizer

For arbitrary \(F\) and a locally bijective \(g \in J.W\), the right-whiskered \(g \triangleright F\) again lies in \(J.W\).

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 416

[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer] With notation as in 415: for an arbitrary presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in \(J.W\):

\[ g \in J.W \; \Longrightarrow \; F \triangleleft g \in J.W, \qquad g \in J.W \; \Longrightarrow \; g \triangleright F \in J.W . \]

These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (20.2) for the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of 417. No flatness and no local triviality is used — they are the flatness-free replacements for the flat variants of 412.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 46

[Linear lifting of adjoint functors] Let \(F \dashv G\) be an adjunction between \(R\)-linear preadditive categories. If \(G\) is \(R\)-linear, then \(F\) is \(R\)-linear.

LaTeX Lean
Lemma 47

[Right-adjoint version] Dual of 46: if \(F\) is \(R\)-linear, then \(G\) is \(R\)-linear.

LaTeX Lean
Proposition 231

[A morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant]

Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition 3.10 (case \(V = \mathbb P^1\)). Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and \(A\) an abelian variety over \(\bar k\). Then every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant: \(f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})\) is a single \(\bar k\)-point.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 233

[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-\(0\) curve to an abelian variety is constant]

Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field (arbitrary characteristic; no \(\mathtt{CharZero}\) hypothesis). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(\mathrm{genus}(C) = 0\), and let \(A\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\) (an abelian variety) with identity \(\eta _A\). Then for every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) such that \(p \mathbin {\, ;\, }f = \eta _A\) for some \(\bar k\)-point \(p \colon \mathbf1 \to C\), the morphism \(f\) equals the constant morphism at the identity, \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\, C \mathbin {\, ;\, }\eta _A\). This is the project’s committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-\(0\) Albanese-witness \(\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}\) consumes. Its signature mirrors \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_ over\_ kbar}\) of 14 verbatim except that the \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\) instance is dropped: same curve typeclasses (\(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\) on \(C\)), same abelian-variety typeclasses (\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\) on \(A\)), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\, C \mathbin {\, ;\, }\eta [A]\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 775

[Albanese universal property of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) – Milne III §6, Proposition 6.1]

Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition 6.1, §III.6, p. 104.

Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g {\gt} 0\) over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point, and write \(J := \operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota _{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the Abel–Jacobi morphism of 783. Then for every abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi (P_0) = \eta _A\), there exists a unique homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota _{P_0}\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 323Albanese universal property of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) (Milne III Prop 6.1)

Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, III §6, Proposition 6.1 (p. 104), with Proposition 6.4 and Remark 6.5 (p. 105). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(g \geq 1\) over \(k\), fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), write \(J := \operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) for the identity component of its Picard scheme, and let \(f^P \colon C \to J\), \(Q \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]\), be the Abel–Jacobi morphism. For any morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to an abelian variety \(A\) over \(k\) with \(\varphi (P) = \eta _A\), there exists a unique homomorphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) of group schemes such that \(\varphi = \psi \circ f^P\). Equivalently, \(J\) together with \(f^P\) exhibits \(J\) as the Albanese object of the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of 304.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 727

[Auslander–Buchsbaum formula]

Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf. Matsumura, Commutative Ring Theory, Theorem 19.1; Auslander–Buchsbaum, Homological dimension in local rings, 1957. Let \((R, \mathfrak {m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then

\[ \text{pd}_R(M) \; +\; \text{depth}(M) \; =\; \text{depth}(R). \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 711

[Milne Theorem 3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target] Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 3.1, §I.3, p. 16. Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (672) has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free (674).

Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free “for free” (i.e. no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\). The extension is unique because \(X\) is reduced and \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\) is dense in \(X\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 822

[The degree map is a group homomorphism] Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). The degree map of 821 is a group homomorphism

\[ \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z, \qquad \deg (D_1 + D_2) \; =\; \deg (D_1) + \deg (D_2), \qquad \deg (-D) \; =\; -\deg (D). \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 857

[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]

Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem 1.3, p. 295. Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then

\[ \chi (\mathcal O_C(D)) \; =\; \deg (D) + 1 - g. \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 365

[Albanese property of the Jacobian]

Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over \(k\) — i.e. a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\). For every pointed morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) with \(f \circ P = \eta [A]\), there exists a unique group-scheme morphism \(g \colon \mathrm{Jac}(C) \to A\) such that \(f = \alpha _P \circ g\).

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} C & \stackrel{f}{\longrightarrow } & A \\ {\scriptstyle \alpha _P} \downarrow & \nearrow {\scriptstyle \exists ! g} & \\ \mathrm{Jac}(C) & & \end{array} \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 625

[FGA representability of the Picard scheme] Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §4, Main Thm. 4.8 + Cor. 4.18.3. Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over \(k\). Then the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\) of 19 is representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\), separated and locally of finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) is called the Picard scheme of \(C/k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 40

[Module-finiteness of the top-section map of a universally closed morphism]

Let \(K\) be a commutative ring and \(X\) an integral scheme equipped with a universally closed, locally-of-finite-type morphism \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec}K\). Then the ring map on top sections \(f^\sharp _{\top }\) is module-finite.

Provided by Mathlib.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 602

[Flat base change of cohomology] Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology). Let

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} X’ & \xrightarrow {g’} & X \\ \downarrow f’ & & \downarrow f \\ S’ & \xrightarrow {g} & S \end{array} \]

be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):

  1. the base-change map \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;

  2. if \(S = \operatorname{Spec}A\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}B\), then \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes _A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 145

[Flat base change, \(i \geq 1\) case] Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (“Flat base change”), the \(i \geq 1\) case. Consider the cartesian square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} X’ & \xrightarrow {\; g’\; } & X \\[2pt] {\scriptstyle f’}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow {\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt] S’ & \xrightarrow {\; g\; } & S \end{array} \]

with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then for every \(i \geq 1\) the canonical base-change map (the higher-degree analogue of the pushforward base-change map of Definition 122) is an isomorphism

\[ g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \; \xrightarrow {\; \sim \; }\; R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}' . \]

Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname {Spec}(A)\), \(S' = \operatorname {Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of \(B\)-modules

\[ H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes _A B \; \xrightarrow {\; \sim \; }\; H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \]

is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times _{\operatorname {Spec}(A)} \operatorname {Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of \(\mathcal{F}\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 141

[Flat base change, \(i=0\) case] Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (“Flat base change”), the \(i = 0\) case. Consider the cartesian square

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} X’ & \xrightarrow {\; g’\; } & X \\[2pt] {\scriptstyle f’}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow {\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt] S’ & \xrightarrow {\; g\; } & S \end{array} \]

with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of Definition 122 is an isomorphism

\[ g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \; \xrightarrow {\; \sim \; }\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' . \]

Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname {Spec}(A)\), \(S' = \operatorname {Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map

\[ H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes _A B \; \xrightarrow {\; \sim \; }\; H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \]

is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times _{\operatorname {Spec}(A)} \operatorname {Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of \(\mathcal{F}\).

The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction (Lemma 140) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be an isomorphism.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 583

[Existence of the flattening stratification]

Source: [Nitsure], §4, Theorem “flattening stratification”; cf. [Stacks Project], tag 052H. Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a coherent sheaf on the relative projective space \(\mathbb {P}^n_S\) over \(S\). Then:

  1. The set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi (\mathbb {P}^n_s, \mathcal{F}_s(m))\) that arise as \(s\) ranges over \(S\) is finite.

  2. For each \(f \in I\) there exists a locally-closed subscheme \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set \(|S_f|\) consists of exactly those \(s \in S\) with \(P_s = f\). The collection \(\{ |S_f|\} _{f \in I}\) is pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union \(|S|\). Forming the coproduct \(S' := \coprod _{f \in I} S_f\) and the morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the inclusions \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\), the pullback \(i^* \mathcal{F}\) on \(\mathbb {P}^n_{S'}\) is flat over \(S'\), and \(i : S' \to S\) is universal with this property: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) such that \(\varphi ^* \mathcal{F}\) on \(\mathbb {P}^n_T\) is flat over \(T\), \(\varphi \) factors uniquely through \(i\). Each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).

  3. Order \(I\) by \(f {\lt} g \iff f(n) {\lt} g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\). Then the closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is contained in \(\bigcup _{g \ge f} |S_g|\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 590

[Universal property of the flattening stratification]

Source: [Nitsure], §4 (part (ii) of the main theorem); cf. [Stacks Project], tag 052H. Let \(S\), \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(\{ S_f\} _{f \in I}\) be as in 583. Set \(S' := \coprod _{f \in I} S_f\) with the coproduct morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the locally-closed inclusions. Then \(i^* \mathcal{F}\) is flat over \(S'\), and the pair \((S', i)\) is universal: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) of noetherian schemes (one may take \(T\) arbitrary, the Noetherian hypothesis on \(S\) being preserved), the pullback \(\varphi ^* \mathcal{F}\) on \(\mathbb {P}^n_T\) is \(T\)-flat if and only if \(\varphi \) factors uniquely through \(i\). In particular each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 582

[Generic flatness, geometric form]

Source: [Nitsure], §4, Theorem “generic flatness”. Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism, and \(\mathcal{F}\) a coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-flat.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 581Generic flatness, algebraic form

Source: [Nitsure], §4, “Lemma on Generic Flatness”. Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 926

[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)] Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example 1.3.5, p. 297. Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\). Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes

\[ \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim }{\longrightarrow } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}. \]

Concretely, the Lean signature AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1 (the existing pinned target at AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290) is closed by the proof below; the existing interface block chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso of chap:avr_for_rr is its consumer-side restatement, pinning the same Lean declaration.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 174

[domain structure on \(\mathrm{Away}\, f \otimes _{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\)] Let \(\overline{k}\) be a field, let \(d \ge 1\), and let \(f\) be a non-zero homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d\) in the \(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading on \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\, 2)\; \overline{k}\). Then the tensor product

\[ \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\bigl(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading}\, \overline{k}\bigr)\, f \; \otimes _{\overline{k}}\; \mathrm{GmRing}\; \overline{k} \]

is a domain (and hence reduced).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 596

[Representability of the Grassmannian]

Source: [Nitsure], §1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch. 5). Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of 595 is representable by a smooth projective \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\), equipped with a tautological quotient \(\pi ^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant line bundle \(\det (\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the associated linear system gives a closed embedding \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow \mathbb {P}_S\! \left(\bigwedge ^d V\right)\) – the Plücker embedding.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 325

[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)] Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If

\[ U.\iota \circ g_1 \; =\; U.\iota \circ g_2 \]

as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 865

[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology] Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition 2.5, p. 208. Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\; (\mathrm{Opens}.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\; X)\; (\mathrm{Module}\bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),

\[ \operatorname{HModule}\; \bar k\; \mathcal F\; i \; =\; 0. \]

In particular, \(\dim _{\bar k} \operatorname{HModule}\; \bar k\; \mathcal F\; 1 = 0\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 4

[\(\operatorname{Ext}\) on the topology of opens, abelian-group valued]

Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then the category of sheaves of abelian groups on the topology of opens of \(X\) admits \(\operatorname{Ext}\) groups.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 8

[\(\operatorname{Ext}\) on the topology of opens, \(k\)-module valued]

Let \(k\) be a commutative ring and \(X\) a topological space. Then the category of sheaves of \(k\)-modules on the topology of opens of \(X\) admits \(\operatorname{Ext}\) groups.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 1

[Sheaf condition transports along the structure-sheaf forget composite] Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then the Grothendieck topology of opens of \(X\) transports sheaves along the forgetful composite from commutative rings to abelian groups.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 3

[Sheafification on the topology of opens, abelian-group valued] Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then the Grothendieck topology of opens of \(X\) admits sheafification for sheaves valued in the category of abelian groups.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 7

[Sheafification on the topology of opens, \(k\)-module valued] Let \(k\) be a commutative ring and \(X\) a topological space. Then the Grothendieck topology of opens of \(X\) admits sheafification for sheaves valued in the category of \(k\)-modules.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 649

[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Lem. 5.1 (3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at 646. Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of 646 along \(\operatorname{Spec}K \to \operatorname{Spec}k\) and using the universal property of the identity component on \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 648

[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Lem. 5.1 (3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at 646. Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over \(k\) — equivalently, locally of finite type and quasi-compact — and geometrically irreducible: after base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 647

[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Lem. 5.1 (3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at 646. Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (646) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 646Identity component is a clopen subscheme

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Lem. 5.1 (3). Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) (645) is an open and closed subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 309

[Uniqueness of the Albanese object] Let \(J_1, J_2\) be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve \((C, P)\), with universal pointed morphisms \(\iota _1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1\) and \(\iota _2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2\) (Definition 306). Then there is a unique morphism \(e \colon J_1 \to J_2\) satisfying \(\iota _2 = \iota _1 \circ e\). The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation \(\mathrm{id}\), and the produced morphism \(e\) is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of \(e\) as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark 310).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 173

[lift through closed immersion via range containment] Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of \(i\):

\[ (\exists \, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g) \; \iff \; \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}). \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 801

[\(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) descends along open immersion] Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) and \(U \hookrightarrow X\) an open subscheme with \(\texttt{IsIntegral}\, U.\texttt{toScheme}\) (the latter is not automatic from integrality of \(X\); it is threaded explicitly). Then \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) also satisfies \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\); the DVR-of-stalk property at a codim-1 point \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) is transported from the corresponding point \(\texttt{ofOpen}\, U\, Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) on the ambient scheme via the stalk isomorphism (800) together with \(\texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing}\) on the ring-equiv image.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 315

[Geometric irreducibility] Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Lem. (lem:agps), Prp. (prp:pic0). \(\mathrm{Jac}(C) \to \operatorname{Spec}k\) is geometrically irreducible.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 312

[Group object structure] \(\mathrm{Jac}(C)\) carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 314

[Properness] Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Prp. (prp:P0). \(\mathrm{Jac}(C) \to \operatorname{Spec}k\) is proper.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 313

[Smoothness and dimension] Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Cor. (cor:sm), Cor. (cor:ch0), Prp. (prp:P0). \(\mathrm{Jac}(C) \to \operatorname{Spec}k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 576

[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow \) finitely presented] Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules, Lemma 17.25.4). Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying 570 and let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in Mathlib’s \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 900

[\(\dim _{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]

Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem 1.3, p. 295 (the \(\chi \)-identity inside the proof) and Example 1.3.5, p. 297 (the genus-\(0\) specialisation chain). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then

\[ \dim _{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \; =\; 2. \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 902Existence of a non-constant rational function from \(\dim H^0 = 2\)

Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a closed point of coheight \(1\), and suppose \(\dim _{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). Then there exists a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\operatorname {ord}_Q(f) \ge 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \ne P\), \(\operatorname {ord}_P(f) \ge -1\), and with nonzero principal divisor \(\div (f) \ne 0\). This is the genus-free substrate carrying the linear-algebra and principal-divisor content of 909, isolated from the genus hypothesis (only the dimension count is consumed).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 901The Euler-characteristic bridge \(H^0 - H^1 = 2\) at \(D = [P]\)

Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point of coheight \(1\). Then, as integers,

\[ \dim _{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \, -\, \dim _{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \; =\; 2. \]

This is the specialisation of the Euler-characteristic identity \(\chi (\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg (D) + 1 - g(C)\) to the single-point divisor \(D = [P]\), with \(\deg ([P]) = 1\) and \(g(C) = 0\); it isolates the \(\chi \)-arithmetic consumed by 900.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 319

[Existence of an Albanese variety uniformly over the marked point]

Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Exercise (ex:jac). Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field \(k\). There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) of relative dimension \(g(C)\) such that, for every \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) is an Albanese object for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition 304. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for \(C\) (Definition 316) is non-empty.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 670

[Geometric irreducibility of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\)]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Prp. 5.3 (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the identity). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) (662) is geometrically irreducible: after base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme \((\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) is irreducible.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 671

[\(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]

Source: [Milne], “Abelian Varieties” (course notes, 2008), §I.1, p. 8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Rmk. 5.26 (the Jacobian \(J := \operatorname{Pic}^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a projective abelian \(S\)-scheme). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) (662) is an abelian variety over \(k\) of dimension \(g\): the underlying \(k\)-scheme is smooth (668), proper (669), and geometrically irreducible (670), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure inherited from \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) via the inclusion \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) (647). In particular, \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne §I.1, Cor. 1.4).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 669

[Properness of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\)]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Thm. 5.4; cf. [Milne], “Abelian Varieties” (course notes, 2008), §I.6 (abelian varieties are projective, p. 27). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) (662) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper: the structural morphism \((\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}) \to \operatorname{Spec}k\) is a projective morphism, hence satisfies the universal closedness and separatedness conditions of properness.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 668

[Smoothness of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\)]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Cor. 5.13 + Cor. 5.14; cf. [Milne], “Abelian Varieties” (course notes, 2008), §III.1, Rmk. 1.4(e), p. 86 (dimension of \(J\) equals the genus). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) (662) is smooth over \(k\) of dimension \(g\): the structural morphism \((\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}) \to \operatorname{Spec}k\) is smooth, and every closed point of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) lies in a smooth open neighbourhood.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 667

[Tangent space at the identity: \(\textup T_0 \operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Thm. 5.11; cf. [Hartshorne], “Algebraic Geometry”, GTM 52, IV.1.1 (genus def. \(g(C) = \dim _k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus \(g = g(C)\) over a field \(k\), and let \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) (662) be the identity component of the Picard scheme of \(C/k\). There is a canonical isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces

\[ \textup T_0 \operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k} \; \cong \; H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C). \]

In particular, the \(k\)-dimension of \(\textup T_0 \operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus \(g(C) = \dim _k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) (171).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 487

[The invertibility-carrier Picard group is abelian] Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition definition-pic and Lemma lemma-constructions-invertible: the isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules form an abelian group under tensor product, with unit \(\mathcal{O}_X\). Let \(X\) be a scheme. The carrier \(\operatorname{Pic}X\) of 483 is an abelian group, with

\[ [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes _X M'], \qquad 1 := [\mathcal{O}_X], \qquad [M]^{-1} := [N] \ \text{for any witness } M \otimes _X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X. \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 627

[\(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §2, Def. 2.2 + §4, Def. 4.1 (the Picard scheme). Let \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of 625. Then \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) is canonically an abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\) and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}(T)\) (well defined because both operations preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup \(H_T = \pi _T^*\operatorname{Pic}(T)\) of 389) are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms \(m : \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k} \times _k \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k} \to \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\), \(i : \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k} \to \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\), and \(e : \operatorname{Spec}k \to \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 665

[Dimension of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]

Source: [Milne], “Abelian Varieties” (course notes, 2008), §III.1, Rmk. 1.4(e), p. 86; cf. [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Cor. 5.13 + Ex. 5.23. Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus \(g = g(C)\) (171) over a field \(k\). The dimension of the abelian variety \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) (664) equals the genus:

\[ \dim _k \operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k} \; =\; g(C). \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 664\(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §5, Ex. 5.23 + Rmk. 5.26; cf. [Milne], “Abelian Varieties”, §I.1 (def. of abelian variety, p. 8). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) of 662 is an abelian variety over \(k\): it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the Lean encoding this is the conjunction of [IsProper], [Smooth], and [GeometricallyIrreducible] on the structural morphism (Pic0Scheme C).hom together with the existence of a [GrpObj] structure on \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity follows automatically from Milne §I.1, Cor. 1.4 and is not separately stated.)

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 666

[\(k\)-points of \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]

Source: [Milne], “Abelian Varieties” (course notes, 2008), §III.1, p. 88 (“\(\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)\)… degree zero”); the verbatim source quote is at 663. Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of the identity component \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\) (646 applied to \(G = \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\), specialised in 662) if and only if the degree \(\deg (\lambda ) \in \mathbb {Z}\) of 663 vanishes. Equivalently, \(\operatorname{Pic}^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker \bigl(\deg : \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb {Z}\bigr)\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 919Degree of the pole divisor equals the function-field degree

For a non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) of smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) (with \(K(C)\) finite over \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) via the \(\varphi \)-induced algebra structure), the degree of the pole divisor equals the function-field extension degree:

\[ \deg \! \bigl(\varphi ^*[\infty ]\bigr) \; =\; [\, K(C) : K(\mathbb P^1)\, ] \; =\; \operatorname {finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C). \]

This is the precise form of the degree identity packaged existentially by 915.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 834

[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve] Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary 6.10, p. 138. Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times }\) the associated principal divisor has degree zero:

\[ \deg (\mathrm{div}(f)) \; =\; 0 \; \in \; \mathbb Z. \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 833

[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism] Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p. 131 (group-homomorphism remark). Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times } \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group of Weil divisors. Concretely

\[ \mathrm{div}(fg) \; =\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g), \qquad \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \; =\; -\mathrm{div}(f), \qquad \mathrm{div}(1) \; =\; 0. \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 842Projective line over \(\bar k\) is regular in codimension one

The scheme \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) underlying \(\texttt{ProjectiveLineBar}\, \bar k\) satisfies \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) (791): every codimension-one stalk is a discrete valuation ring.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 390

[Pullback descends through the quotient]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §2, Defs. df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word functor encodes naturality in \(T\); cf. Stacks tag 01HG). Let \(C/k\) be as in 382, and let \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps \(g_C^* : \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T) \to \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T')\) and \(g^* : \operatorname{Pic}(T) \to \operatorname{Pic}(T')\) make the diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{Pic}(T) & \xrightarrow {\; \; \pi _T^*\; \; } & \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T) \\ \; \downarrow \, g^* & & \; \downarrow \, g_C^* \\ \operatorname{Pic}(T’) & \xrightarrow {\; \; \pi _{T'}^*\; \; } & \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T’) \end{array} \]

commute (388). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends \(H_T = \pi _T^*\operatorname{Pic}(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi _{T'}^*\operatorname{Pic}(T')\) and hence induces a unique set map

\[ g^\sharp \; \; \colon \; \; \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T) \; \longrightarrow \; \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T'), \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \; \longmapsto \; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}], \]

through the quotients of 389. The assignment \(g \mapsto g^\sharp \) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp \) for any composable \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus \(T \mapsto \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k} : (\operatorname{Sch}/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 604

[Stalkwise base-change isomorphism on affines] For every affine open \(V \subseteq S'\) and every affine open \(U \subseteq X\) lying over \(V\), the local component \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square ).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\) of 603 is an isomorphism. Proof reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine morphism.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 607Open-cover descent for the stalkwise base-change isomorphism

Suppose the section of the canonical base-change map (603) is an isomorphism over every affine open of \(S'\). Then it is an isomorphism over every open \(U \subseteq S'\). This is the standard Mayer-Vietoris descent for a morphism of quasi-coherent sheaves on the base: cover \(U\) by affine opens, on each of which the section is an isomorphism by hypothesis, and glue along the intersections (quasi-compact because \(f\) is quasi-separated). It is the descent half of the stalkwise statement 604, whose Lean proof feeds the affine-open case 606 as the affine-open hypothesis.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 605Affine-base case of the stalkwise base-change isomorphism

Assume in addition that the base \(S\) is affine, that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, that \(g\) is flat, and that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent. Then for every affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) the section of the canonical base-change map (603) over \(U\) is an isomorphism. Taking \(V = S\) (affine) as the compatible base open, the statement reduces to the algebraic flat base-change identity: the global sections \(\Gamma (S', U)\) are flat over \(\Gamma (S, S)\), and the tensor-presentation of the pulled-back sections (610, applied to the pushforward \((\pi _f)_* \mathcal{F}\), which stays quasi-coherent by 617) matches the base-change map under the Stacks 01HQ identification of 616.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 606Affine-open case of the stalkwise base-change isomorphism

With \(f\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(g\) flat, and \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent (but \(S\) now arbitrary), the section of the canonical base-change map (603) over an affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) is an isomorphism. The substantive algebraic content is the affine-base specialization (605); the passage from a general base \(S\) to the affine case is a base-side Mayer-Vietoris descent along a finite affine cover of \(S\), using the definitional section identity 608 on each piece.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 609

[Canonical base-change map is a sheaf isomorphism] Globalising the stalk-wise statement 604: the natural transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \to f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\) of 603 is an isomorphism of quasi-coherent sheaves of modules. This is the sheaf-level form of 602(1).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 613The base map is a base change

For compatible affine opens \(U \subseteq Y\), \(V \subseteq X\) of a morphism \(g : Y \to X\) and a quasi-coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{N}\) on \(X\), the base map of 612 exhibits \(\Gamma (g^* \mathcal{N}, U)\) as the base change of \(\Gamma (\mathcal{N}, V)\) along the ring map \(\Gamma (X, V) \to \Gamma (Y, U)\); i.e. the induced \(\Gamma (Y, U) \otimes _{\Gamma (X, V)} \Gamma (\mathcal{N}, V) \to \Gamma (g^* \mathcal{N}, U)\) is an isomorphism. The proof feeds the packaged section-level linear equivalence and its intertwining property (615) into the universal characterization of a base change.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 614Existence of the tensor-presentation equivalence

In the same affine setup, there exists a \(\Gamma (Y, U)\)-linear isomorphism

\[ \Gamma \bigl(g^* \mathcal{N},\ U\bigr) \; \cong \; \Gamma (Y, U) \otimes _{\Gamma (X, V)} \Gamma (\mathcal{N}, V), \]

obtained from the base-change property (613) by passing to the induced equivalence. This is the existence statement consumed by the tensor-presentation iso 610.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 597

[Representability of the Quot functor]

Source: [Nitsure], §5, Theorem (Grothendieck), Theorem (Altman–Kleiman) (FGA Explained Ch. 5); cf. Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV. Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\Phi \in \mathbb {Q}[\lambda ]\). Then the functor \(\operatorname {Quot}^{\Phi ,L}_{E/X/S}\) of 594 is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\operatorname {Quot}^{\Phi ,L}_{E/X/S}\), equipped with a universal quotient

\[ q^{\mathrm{univ}} : \pi ^*_{\operatorname {Quot}} E \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}^{\mathrm{univ}} \quad \text{on } X \times _S \operatorname {Quot}^{\Phi ,L}_{E/X/S}, \]

flat over \(\operatorname {Quot}^{\Phi ,L}_{E/X/S}\) with constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi \) on every fiber. The Hilbert scheme is the special case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\): \(\operatorname {Hilb}^{\Phi ,L}_{X/S} = \operatorname {Quot}^{\Phi ,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) represents the functor of \(T\)-flat closed subschemes \(Y \subset X_T\) with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi \).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 768

[Milne Theorem 3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]

Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 3.2, §I.3, p. 17.

Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi _U\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 488

[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\mathrm{Scheme}.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §2, Defs. df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field \(k\), let \(\pi _C : C \to \operatorname{Spec}k\) be the structure morphism, and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism \(\pi _T : T \to \operatorname{Spec}k\). The set

\[ \operatorname{Pic}^{\sharp }_{C/k}(T) \; \; :=\; \; \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T) \, /\, \pi _T^*\operatorname{Pic}(T) \]

of 389 carries a canonical abelian-group structure with addition \(\bigl[\, \mathcal{L}\, \bigr] + \bigl[\, \mathcal{L}'\, \bigr] := \bigl[\, \mathcal{L} \otimes _{C \times _k T} \mathcal{L}'\, \bigr]\), neutral element \(\bigl[\, \mathcal{O}_{C \times _k T}\, \bigr]\), and inverse \(-\bigl[\, \mathcal{L}\, \bigr] := \bigl[\, \mathcal{L}^{-1}\, \bigr]\), where \(\otimes _{C \times _k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations supplied by the substrate of 403 restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\, \pi _C\, \pi _T\) carrier via 422. With this structure the quotient map \(\operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T) \twoheadrightarrow \operatorname{Pic}^{\sharp }_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi _T^*\operatorname{Pic}(T)\), in agreement with 391.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 401

[Étale sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §2, Def. df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following (the étale sheaf \(\operatorname{Pic}_{(X/S)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\) is named together with the abelian-group-valued relative Picard functor, meaning the sheaf is taken in the category of abelian groups, and is universal among such sheaves receiving a presheaf morphism from \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\)). This block records the bare existence of a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups

\[ \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k} \; \; \longrightarrow \; \; \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}} \]

inside the category of presheaves \((\operatorname{Sch}/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{AddCommGroup}\); equivalently, the hom-set of presheaf morphisms from \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}\) to \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\) is nonempty. The full canonical statement — that this morphism is the canonical sheafification unit and carries the universal property characterising \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\) as the initial étale sheaf of abelian groups receiving a presheaf morphism from \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}\), so in particular that the abelian-group structure on \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}(T)\) is uniquely determined — is recorded separately as 402 below.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 402Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §2, Def. df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) into étale sheaves of abelian groups). Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups

\[ \eta \; \colon \; \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k} \; \longrightarrow \; \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}} \]

with the following universal property. For every étale sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\operatorname{Sch}/k)\) and every morphism of presheaves of abelian groups \(\varphi : \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique morphism of étale sheaves of abelian groups \(\tilde\varphi : \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi \). Equivalently, the pair \(\bigl(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}, \, \eta \bigr)\) is initial among pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\) consisting of an étale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\operatorname{Sch}/k)\) and a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}\) to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{(C/k)\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group structure on \(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of \(\eta \).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 399

[\(\operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}\) is a group-valued presheaf]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §2, Defs. df:aPf + df:Pfs. The assignment \(T \mapsto \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T)\) of 397, together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp \) of 398, defines a presheaf of abelian groups

\[ \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k} \; \; \colon \; \; (\operatorname{Sch}/k)^{op} \; \longrightarrow \; \mathrm{AddCommGroup} \]

on the category of \(k\)-schemes.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 389

[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]

Source: [Kleiman], “The Picard scheme”, §2, Def. “relative Picard functor” df:Pfs. Let \(C/k\) be as in 382 and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. The subset

\[ H_T \; \; :=\; \; \pi _T^* \operatorname{Pic}(T) \; \; \subseteq \; \; \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T) \]

is the image of the pullback map \(\pi _T^*\) (387); in particular it contains the trivial class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T)\) (so it is a subgroup). Define

\[ \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T) \; \; :=\; \; \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T) \, / \, H_T, \]

the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T)\) under the relation \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\). Then \(T \mapsto \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map \(\operatorname{Pic}(C \times _k T) \to \operatorname{Pic}^\sharp _{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 370

[Relative spectrum over an affine base]

Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine). Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec}R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma (X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes

\[ \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A}) \; \; \cong \; \; \operatorname{Spec}(A). \]

In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute spectrum of the global sections.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 371

[Base change of the relative spectrum]

Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LS (lemma-spec-base-change + lemma-spec-properties). Let \(g : T \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Then \(g^* \mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebra and there is a canonical isomorphism of \(T\)-schemes

\[ T \times _X \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A}) \; \; \cong \; \; \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_T(g^* \mathcal{A}). \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 367

[Relative spectrum exists]

Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec); cf. [Hartshorne], II Ex. 5.17(a). Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra (366). There exists a scheme \(\underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism \(\pi : \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that

  1. for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism \(i_U : \pi ^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow {\sim } \operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);

  2. for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow {i_U^{-1}} \pi ^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow \pi ^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow {i_{U'}} \operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).

The pair \((\underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi )\) is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(X\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 381

[Relative spectrum as a functor]

Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec + lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties); [Hartshorne], II Ex. 5.17(b). The construction \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A})\) assembles into a contravariant functor

\[ \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X \]

from quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras to affine \(X\)-schemes: a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras \(\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) induces, by the universal property (369) applied to the pair \((\pi _\mathcal {B}, \psi \circ \pi _\mathcal {B}^*) : \pi _\mathcal {B}^*\mathcal{A} \to \pi _\mathcal {B}^*\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathcal{B}}\), an \(X\)-morphism \(\underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\psi ) : \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A})\). Functoriality (identity, composition) is direct from the universal property. The pushforward \(\pi _* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}\) exhibits \(\underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X\) as an equivalence between \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}\) and the full subcategory of affine \(X\)-schemes, with inverse \(f \mapsto f_* \mathcal{O}_Y\) (Stacks lemma-spec-properties part (3)).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 369

[Universal property of the relative spectrum]

Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec functor description); [Hartshorne], II Ex. 5.17(b). Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Then \(\underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor \(F : \operatorname{Sch}^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\), \(T \mapsto F(T) := \{ (f, \varphi ) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\; \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\} .\) Equivalently, for any \(X\)-scheme \(g : T \to X\), there is a natural bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_X(T, \underline{\operatorname{Spec}}_X(\mathcal{A})) \; \; \cong \; \; \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T) \]

(using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)). The bijection is natural in \(T\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 858

[Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero]

Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example 1.3.5, p. 297. Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with \(\deg (D) \geq 0\). Then

\[ \ell (D) \; =\; \deg (D) + 1. \]
LaTeX Lean
Lemma 175

[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]

Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4, Rigidity Lemma (Form I), p. 43. Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image \(f(X \times \{ y_0\} )\) is a single point \(z_0 \in Z\). Then there is a morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any fixed \(x_0 \in X\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 327

Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, II §4. Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero (Lean: [IsAlgClosed kbar] \(+\) [CharZero kbar]; in the use case, the algebraic closure of a base field \(k\), with descent of the conclusion to \(k\) handled downstream in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). These two hypotheses match the theorem’s existing docstring and are required by the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow \) constancy chain (355, 351); see §"Iter-152 alg-closed pivot". Let \(C : \mathrm{Over}\, (\operatorname{Spec}\bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(\mathrm{genus}(C) = 0\) (i.e. \(\dim _{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\)). Let \(A : \mathrm{Over}\, (\operatorname{Spec}\bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\), with identity element \(\eta _A \in A(\bar k)\). Let

\[ f \colon C \longrightarrow A \]

be a morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes over \(\operatorname{Spec}\bar k\), and let \(p \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point such that \(f(p) = \eta _A\). Then \(f\) equals the constant morphism at \(\eta _A\):

\[ f \; =\; \bigl(C \to \operatorname{Spec}\bar k \xrightarrow {\, \eta _A\, } A\bigr). \]

Encoding note (iter-126). The Lean signature uses the abstract characterization \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{IsProper}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathrm{genus}(C) = 0\) instead of a literal \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) scheme. The two encodings are equivalent over \(\bar k\) once the curve has a \(\bar k\)-rational point (Brauer–Severi triviality + Riemann–Roch on \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)); the abstract form is used because Mathlib snapshot b80f227 does not ship a packaged \(\mathtt{ProjectiveSpace}\, n\, S\) as a \(\mathtt{Scheme.Over}\, S\), and the C.2.b–C.2.d proof decomposition below works for any smooth proper geometrically integral genus-0 curve over \(\bar k\) without explicit \(\mathbb P^1\) API.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 721

[Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]

Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then

\[ \operatorname {ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \; =\; \operatorname {coheight}_{X}(z), \]

i.e. the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation preorder).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 106

[Finite rank via the \(X_4\) corner, abstract sheaf form]

Let \(S\) be a two-affine cover of \(X\) and \(\mathcal F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. Then

\[ \dim _k H^{n}(X, \mathcal F) \; =\; \dim _k H^{\prime n}(S.\mathrm{toMayerVietorisSquare}.X_4, \mathcal F). \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 107

[Finite rank via the \(X_4\) corner, curve form]

Let \(C\) be a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\) and \(S\) a two-affine cover of \(C\). Then

\[ \dim _k H^{n}(C, \mathcal O_C) \; =\; \dim _k H^{\prime n}(S.\mathrm{toMayerVietorisSquare}.X_4, \mathcal O_C). \]
LaTeX Lean
Theorem 96

[Exactness for the structure sheaf on a curve]

The sequence of 95 is exact.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 94

[Exactness on a two-affine cover]

The sequence of 93 is exact.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 108

[Finite-dimensionality via the \(X_4\) corner, abstract sheaf form]

Let \(S\) be a two-affine cover of \(X\) and \(\mathcal F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. If \(H^{\prime n}(S.\mathrm{toMayerVietorisSquare}.X_4, \mathcal F)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\), then so is \(H^{n}(X, \mathcal F)\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 109

[Finite-dimensionality via the \(X_4\) corner, curve form]

Let \(C\) be a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\) and \(S\) a two-affine cover of \(C\). If \(H^{\prime n}(S.\mathrm{toMayerVietorisSquare}.X_4, \mathcal O_C)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\), then so is \(H^{n}(C, \mathcal O_C)\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 55

[Čech complex bridge to structure-sheaf specialisation] The structure-sheaf Čech complex equals the parameterised Čech complex at \(F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 56

[Čech cohomology bridge to structure-sheaf specialisation] The structure-sheaf Čech cohomology equals the parameterised Čech cohomology at \(F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 83

[Mayer–Vietoris exactness]

The Mayer–Vietoris sequence of 77 is exact.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 121

[Producer for \(\mathtt{IsAffineHModuleVanishing}\)]

If \(\mathtt{HasAffineCechAcyclicCover}\) holds, then \(\mathtt{IsAffineHModuleVanishing}\) holds.

LaTeX Lean
Definition 34

[Affine cohomology vanishing carrier predicate] Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\), and \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules on \(C\). The class of affine cohomology vanishing for \(F\) asserts that for every affine open \(U\) of \(C\) and every degree \(i {\gt} 0\), the open-evaluation cohomology \(H^{\prime i}(U, F)\) is a subsingleton (equivalently, the zero \(k\)-module).

LaTeX Lean
Definition 36

[Wholespace \(H^0\) Hom-finiteness carrier predicate]

Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\), and \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules on \(C\). The class of wholespace \(H^0\) Hom-finiteness asserts that the global morphism group \(\operatorname{Hom}(\text{constant sheaf at }k, F)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 43

[Finite-dimensionality on global-sections object] Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) an integral scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\) with proper structure morphism. Then the global-sections object of the \(k\)-module-valued structure sheaf is finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 41

[Stein finiteness of global sections]

Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) an integral scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\) with proper structure morphism. Then \(\Gamma (C, \mathcal O_C)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 35

[Finite-dimensionality from affine vanishing] Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\), and \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. Assume affine cohomology vanishing holds for \(F\). For any affine open \(U\) of \(C\) and any \(i {\gt} 0\), the cohomology group \(H^{\prime i}(U, F)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 31

[\(k\)-finite transport for open \(H^0\)] Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a Grothendieck site, \(X\) an object of \(C\), and \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. If the \(k\)-linear Hom group \(\operatorname{Hom}(\text{sheafified representable at }X, F)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\), then \(H^{\prime 0}(X, F)\) is also finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 33

[Curve specialisation of open \(H^0\) finite-dimensionality] Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\), and \(U\) an open of \(C\). If the \(k\)-linear Hom group from the sheafified representable at \(U\) to the \(k\)-module-valued structure sheaf is finite-dimensional over \(k\), then \(H^{\prime 0}(U, \mathcal O_C)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 30

[\(k\)-finite transport for global \(H^0\)]

Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a Grothendieck site admitting sheafification of \(k\)-modules and \(\operatorname{Ext}\), and \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. If the \(k\)-linear Hom group \(\operatorname{Hom}(\text{constant sheaf at }k, F)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\), then \(H^0_{\mathrm{Module}}(F)\) is also finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 32

[Curve specialisation of global \(H^0\) finite-dimensionality] Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\). If the \(k\)-linear Hom group from the constant sheaf at \(k\) to the \(k\)-module-valued structure sheaf is finite-dimensional over \(k\), then \(H^0_{\mathrm{Module}}(\mathcal O_C)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 37

[Finite-dimensionality of global \(H^0\) from wholespace Hom-finiteness] Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\), and \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. Assume wholespace \(H^0\) Hom-finiteness holds for \(F\). Then \(H^0_{\mathrm{Module}}(F)\) is finite-dimensional over \(k\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 38

[Curve specialisation] Direct curve specialisation of 37 to \(F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 42

[Global-sections-to-top linear equivalence]

Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) a topological space, and \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules on \(X\). Then there is a \(k\)-linear equivalence between the global-sections object of \(F\) and the evaluation of \(F\) at the top open \(\top \).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 114

[Instance-driven top-supremum transport, abstract sheaf form]

Under Čech-acyclicity, \(\mathtt{HasCechToHModuleIso}\), and \(\bigsqcup \mathfrak U_i = \top \), the type \(H^{n}(X, \mathcal F)\) is a subsingleton for \(n \ge 1\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 115

[Instance-driven top-supremum transport, curve form]

Same statement at \(\mathcal F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 110

[Top-supremum transport, abstract sheaf form]

Let \(\mathfrak U\) be a Čech-acyclic cover with \(\bigsqcup _i \mathfrak U_i = \top \), and assume a comparison isomorphism between Čech and derived cohomology. For \(n \ge 1\), the type \(H^{n}(X, \mathcal F)\) is a subsingleton.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 111

[Top-supremum transport, curve form]

Same statement at \(\mathcal F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 118

[Generalised rewrite-bridge, abstract sheaf form]

Under Čech-acyclicity and \(\mathtt{HasCechToHModuleIso}\), if \(\bigsqcup \mathfrak U_i = U\), then \(H^{\prime n}(U, \mathcal F)\) is a subsingleton for \(n \ge 1\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 119

[Generalised rewrite-bridge, curve form]

Same statement at \(\mathcal F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 116

[Instance-driven supremum transport for \(H'\), abstract sheaf form]

Under Čech-acyclicity and \(\mathtt{HasCechToHModuleIso}\), the type \(H^{\prime n}(\bigsqcup \mathfrak U_i, \mathcal F)\) is a subsingleton for \(n \ge 1\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 117

[Instance-driven supremum transport for \(H'\), curve form]

Same statement at \(\mathcal F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 59

[Derived vanishing from Čech acyclicity]

Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec}k\), \(F\) a sheaf of \(k\)-modules, and \(\mathcal U\) a Čech-acyclic cover for \(F\). Suppose there is a \(k\)-linear comparison isomorphism between Čech cohomology and derived cohomology of the cover supremum for every degree. Then for every \(n \ge 1\), the derived cohomology of the cover supremum is a subsingleton.

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 60

[Curve specialisation]

Curve specialisation of 59 at \(F = \mathcal O_C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 15

[Sheaf condition]

The presheaf of 14 is a sheaf for the Grothendieck topology of opens of \(C\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 163

[Smooth implies \(\Omega \) locally free (algebra-Kähler form)]

Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(n \in \mathbb {N}\). If \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\), then for every point \(x \in X\) there exist an affine open \(U \subseteq S\), an affine open \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\), such that the Kähler differential module

\[ \Omega _{\Gamma (X, V) \, /\, \Gamma (S, U)} \]

(over the appLE algebra structure \(\Gamma (S, U) \to \Gamma (X, V)\) induced by \(f\)) is a free \(\Gamma (X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\).

LaTeX Lean
Theorem 715

[Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction] Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp. 130–131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map). Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\), let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a prime divisor with generic point \(\eta _W \in W\) (so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{ \eta _W\} } = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi _U)\) be any representative of \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset \). The following are equivalent:

  1. \(f\) is defined at \(\eta _W\) (i.e. \(\eta _W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).

  2. For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\) pulls back to a rational function \(\varphi _U^\ast (g) \in K(X)\) satisfying

    \[ \operatorname {ord}_W\bigl(\varphi _U^\ast (g)\bigr) \; \geq \; 0. \]

Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free (674) if and only if, for every prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback \(\varphi _U^\ast (g)\) has \(\operatorname {ord}_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).

LaTeX Lean
